# Results

The analytical corpus comprises 6,833 emails from the inbox of a central CITF Databank
coordinator, spanning correspondence with 100 participating studies from February 2021
to September 2025, involving 488 unique senders. NMF identified 14 topics organized
into four thematic categories: Agreements and Contracts (5 topics, 3,793 multi-topic
email assignments), Data Operations (4 topics, 2,772), Study Documentation (3 topics,
2,602), and Coordination (2 topics, 1,612). Table 1 presents the full list of topics with
their labels, thematic categories, top keywords, and coherence scores. Figure 1 shows
the distribution of emails across all 14 topics.

## What the Emails Were About: Convergence of Legal and Technical Matters

Topic co-occurrence analysis revealed two largely separate clusters of topics within the
corpus (Figure 2). Core legal topics — Document Signatures, Document Appendices, and
DSA Status — co-occurred frequently with each other (co-occurrence scores: 0.19--0.28)
but rarely with core technical topics such as Data Upload Instructions, Variable Mapping,
and Data Transfer Initiation (co-occurrence scores: 0.02--0.09). This structural
separation suggests that governance and data operations workstreams ran largely in
parallel rather than in close coordination.

The primary point of convergence between these two clusters was Document Review and
Comments, the most frequently assigned topic in the corpus (1,323 email assignments)
and the most broadly connected across all thematic categories (co-occurrence scores:
0.10--0.24). This topic likely reflects the reconciliation of technical data descriptions
and management requirements with institutional legal expectations, as data sharing
agreements needed to stipulate the exact data being shared and the conditions under
which they would be managed. It represents the interface between governance and
operations — the point at which legal and technical concerns were most likely to be
addressed together.

## When Topics Appeared: Persistence of Governance

While dominant topics shifted clearly across phases of the data sharing lifecycle, legal
and administrative concerns proved more persistent than the phase structure alone would
suggest (Figure 3). In early phases, correspondence was dominated by legal document
work: DSA Status accounted for 12% of emails and Document Review for 12% in the
Intro-to-DSA phase. In later phases, technical topics came to the fore: Data Upload
Instructions accounted for 27% of emails in the Upload-to-Catalogue phase, and
Serology and Data Harmonization for 30% in the Catalogue-to-Publication phase.

However, multi-topic analysis showed that legal and administrative concerns did not
recede as the lifecycle progressed. Even in the Catalogue-to-Publication phase —
dominated by Serology and Data Harmonization — DSA Status and Amendments (5%),
Document Review (7%), and Document Signatures (5%) together still accounted for 17%
of emails. Rather than being treated as an initial phase with a definite endpoint, data
governance concerns persisted throughout the data sharing lifecycle, requiring ongoing
attention well beyond the execution of formal agreements.

Phase duration data further illustrate where effort was concentrated. Studies spent a
median of 395 days in DSA negotiation (Intro-to-DSA phase) and 398 days in data
preparation (DSA-to-Upload phase), compared to only 22 days for technical cataloguing
(Upload-to-Catalogue phase) — an 18:1 ratio of administrative to technical time. Table 2
presents the full distribution of phase durations across all studies.

## Who Did the Work: Distributions of Labour

Sender analysis revealed sharply uneven operational engagement across institutional
roles (Figure 4). Databank teams were substantially more active as email senders than
representatives of partner studies. The Harmonization Group sent emails at a ratio of
115 emails per sender, and the Secretariat at 101 emails per sender, compared to 7
emails per sender among External Partners and 5 among Study Investigators. This
concentration of email activity within Databank teams makes visible the coordination
labour that is sustained by a small number of highly active individuals.

The topical focus of each sender group also differed markedly. The Harmonization Group
concentrated over 60% of its emails on two topics — Variable Mapping and Serology and
Data Harmonization — reflecting its specialized focus on the technical core of data
interoperability. The Secretariat's topical profile was the most evenly distributed,
reflecting its broad coordinating mandate across all phases and workstreams. Study
Investigators' most frequent topic was Meeting Scheduling, which co-occurred most
strongly with Document Review (0.19) and DSA Status (0.15), suggesting that
investigators were convened primarily to discuss legal and documentation matters rather
than to participate in technical data operations.

Gender analysis revealed a consistent pattern of stratification across both roles and
phases (Figure 5). Women comprised 74% of Study Staff senders — the largest sender
group and the one carrying the greatest volume of operational coordination work —
while the Databank Secretariat was the only category where men were the majority
(57%). At the phase level, women-coded senders dominated the earliest and most
labour-intensive phases: Funding to Introduction (69% women) and Introduction to DSA
(63% women). The final publication phase was the most male-dominated (67% men).
This progression — from female-dominated early phases involving onboarding and
negotiation, to male-dominated later phases associated with publication — is consistent
with prior research documenting the feminization of invisible coordination labour in
research data infrastructure [@choroszewicz2022].
